info.text: Generic font-family names like serif, monospace or sans-serif point to a general style of font, not one specific typeface. A name like Helvetica, on the other hand, is specific.
info.title: font-family
Helvetica
info.text: Helvetica is a very common font, there's even an entire documentary about it, but specific fonts like this will only load if the user already has them installed on their computer. Since not all operating systems come with Helvetica, it's best to follow it with a generic family name, like sans-serif, as a fallback.

info.title: font stacks
info.text: Adding a comma , after a font name followed by another font name is called a CSS font stack. You can include as many fonts in the stack as you want. The browser will always try the first one, and if it isn't available, move down the list. That's why you should always end with a generic family name. You can see more examples of font stacks here.

info.title: font service
info.text: If we want a font the user probably doesn't have, we need to load it explicitly. One common way is to import a stylesheet from a font service like Google Fonts. We can do this a couple of different ways.

info.title: link tag
info.text: We can use a <link> tag to import the stylesheet, as we would with our own stylesheets.

info.title: @import
info.text: Or we could use the CSS @import rule with a url() function that pulls in a stylesheet

info.title: same url
info.text: Both of these point to the same URL, the @import rule with the url() function happens to be how we import a stylesheet from another server in CSS, whereas the <link> tag with the href attribute is how we do it in HTML.

info.title: use imported font
info.text: Once imported, we can use it in any of our CSS rules. Here I've created a CSS class, which I've applied to one of the h1 elements on my page, making use of this custom font.

info.title: dependency
info.text: The downside of this approach is that it makes your site dependent on that service and its policies, which can change at any time. Surveillance capitalism services like Google also collect data such as the IP address and browser of everyone who visits your site when you use their hosted fonts.

info.title: local font
info.text: Some developers are fine with that trade-off, but I prefer protecting my visitors' privacy. So another approach is to import your own font, I've got a font file called "cyberdyne.ttf" saved locally.

info.title: downloading fonts
info.text: You can find loads of free-to-use fonts from sites like Font Squirrel or Dafont, where I downloaded this one. Downloading ttf or woff font file types both work.

info.title: @font-face
info.text: Once we've downloaded a font and included it in our project we can create an @font-face rule for it where we use the font-family to name our loaded font and then the src with the url() function to load our font file.

info.title: use loaded font
info.text: Once loaded, we can use it in any of our CSS rules just like we did our imported font. Here I've created a CSS class with our new font which I've applied to the last h1 on my page.

info.title: quotes
info.text: I've wrapped "cyberdyne" in quotes, but because this font name is only one word I don't technically have to. That's why Helvetica and Arial don't have quotes, but "Lucida Grande" does.
